summary: I come from New Castle, Virginia, where I had rather humble beginnings. It was there that my early life started before I moved on to other places. My roots in New Castle shaped much of my early character and ambitions.

Although my mother worked caring for a sick woman in quarters, it wasn't at Norfolk Naval Hospital but nearby. I went to school in Norfolk but didn't finish high school there. Norfolk was part of my early life and education before I pursued further studies and my Navy career.

The chief petty officer was the man whose wife my mother cared for when I was about 15. We lived in their quarters, and I helped out with medical care during that time. The chief was present and at sea sometimes, and his family was part of my early exposure to Navy life.
cues: mother cared for chief petty officer's wife

summary: My great-grandmother was Canadian, as best as I can recall, and she owned a large pharmacy in Norfolk. Women pharmacists were rare back in the 1800s, so that was a notable part of my family history. This heritage gave me some early inspiration toward the medical field.

My family on my mother's side came from farmland about 8 or 10 miles beyond Suffolk, Virginia. They were involved in farming, particularly peanuts, which was the main thing they had going. Suffolk was part of my family background and roots.

The old Portsmouth Naval Hospital was across the Elizabeth River from Norfolk, and I remember it as a historic place with dungeons in the basement used during the Civil War. The building had Roman architecture and thick deadlights that let light into the dungeons. Though I lived at the air station, I spent time at the dispensary connected to the hospital, helping out and learning medical care.

The Elizabeth River runs by the Portsmouth Naval Hospital, which had a grand facade facing the river with big steps and thick glass deadlights. The river was a geographical marker for the hospital and the area where I spent time during my early medical experiences. It was part of the naval community I grew up around.

After my early schooling and getting my GED, I took a lot of courses at George Washington University to continue my education. It was part of my self-education journey after not finishing high school traditionally. The university helped me broaden my knowledge beyond the Navy training.

I lived at the Portsmouth Naval Air Station and spent most of my time at the dispensary there, where I was sort of adopted as a mascot. I helped polish floors, serve meals, punch capsules, and type, all while learning about medical care. It was a formative place before I enlisted in the Navy.

I always wanted to be in the United States Navy and enlisted when I was 16. The Navy gave me security and education during the Great Depression, which was a time of hardship for many. I trained at Norfolk boot camp, served aboard the USS Nitro, and later attended Hospital Corps School, all shaping my career in the Navy.

I attended the Hospital Corps School in San Diego, located in a beautiful Spanish architecture building near Balboa Park. The school taught anatomy, physiology, pharmacy, nursing, and other medical subjects, preparing me to be a hospital corpsman. I was among the top of my class and valued the education highly during a time when jobs were scarce.

In 1936, I arrived in San Diego after traveling aboard the USS Nitro through the Panama Canal. San Diego was where I went to Hospital Corps School and experienced my first earthquake. The city and the school there were important in my transition from apprentice seaman to hospital corpsman.

I served aboard the USS Nitro as an apprentice seaman while en route from the East Coast to San Diego through the Panama Canal. The Nitro had wooden decks that I scrubbed early in the morning, and I helped maintain the ship during the voyage. It was my first ship assignment after boot camp and before attending Hospital Corps School.

While traveling to San Diego aboard the USS Nitro, we passed through the Panama Canal. I remember standing near the lifeline in the Miraflores locks, taking in the scenery. The canal was a key part of my journey from the East Coast to the West Coast Navy training.

I had a memorable encounter with a boatswain's mate aboard the USS Nitro who was a typical tough Navy man of the time, wearing his hat low and his whistle always around his neck. I unintentionally challenged him by not obeying his order quickly, and he punched me, breaking my nose. Years later, I recognized him as 'Jocko' Martin when I was a warrant officer in Philadelphia, and I thanked him for teaching me a valuable lesson.

Later in my career, I served at the naval hospital in Philadelphia as a warrant officer. It was there I encountered the boatswain's mate who had punched me years before. Philadelphia was an important duty station where I continued my Navy medical service.

The Hospital Corps School was located right across the street from Balboa Park in San Diego. The park was a beautiful landmark near the school, which was set in Spanish architecture buildings with a large swimming pool nearby. It made for an idyllic setting during my medical training.

During the Great Depression, the Navy was a place of security and opportunity for me. While many people had no jobs or homes, the Navy provided education and a future. I viewed the Depression as a time of prosperity for those of us in the service.

The Navy's articles of government had remained very rigid since the time of John Adams, with only a few changes like abolishing keel hauling and flogging. This showed how traditional and strict Navy discipline was during my service. It was a tough environment but one that shaped my character.

A friend of mine who was a pharmacist's mate received a bad conduct discharge for being late but later rejoined the Navy during the war and earned several commendations for valor, including Silver Stars. This showed the extremes of Navy discipline and the opportunities for redemption. The Silver Star represented the highest valor awards some of us aspired to.
